chunk_summary: Adult fibrosis scores like FIB4 and NFS are unsuitable for pediatric MASLD due to age-weighted distortions. Pediatric-specific indices and elastography are preferred for staging, with clinical triggers guiding referrals and biopsies.
learning_objectives: Understand the limitations of adult fibrosis scores in pediatric MASLD diagnosis.
Identify high-concern clinical triggers for fibrosis risk in children.
Learn the role of transient elastography and pediatric-specific indices in MASLD staging.
Recognize the importance of timely referral to pediatric hepatology for advanced evaluation.
key_facts: Adult fibrosis scores (e.g., FIB4, NFS) are unreliable for pediatric MASLD due to age-weighted components.
High-concern triggers include ALT >2× sex-specific upper limit for >3 months, ALT >80 IU/L, hepatosplenomegaly, AST/ALT ratio >1, or comorbid T2DM.
Transient elastography with liver stiffness measurement is the preferred noninvasive staging tool in pediatrics.
Pediatric-specific indices like PNFI exist but lack external validation across populations.
Persistent biochemical elevation plus any high-concern trigger warrants pediatric hepatology evaluation.
reference_quote: Adult indices such as FIB4 and NFS were derived from adult cohorts and rely on age-weighted components that distort performance in pediatrics; pediatric guidance therefore cautions against using them to make referral or biopsy decisions.
document_key_topics: Limitations of adult fibrosis scores in pediatric MASLD
Noninvasive staging tools for pediatric MASLD
Clinical triggers for fibrosis risk in children
Referral and biopsy guidelines for pediatric MASLD
